perturbation.original_text: Vector hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating a possible transaction between the parties, that the Evaluation Material will be kept confidential and that Vector and its Representatives will not disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) Vector may make any disclosure of such information to which Captaris gives its prior written consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed by Vector to its Representatives who need to know such information for the sole purpose of evaluating and implementing a possible transaction between the parties, who shall keep such information confidential and who are provided with a copy of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Vector hereby agrees that it and its Representatives shall use the Evaluation Material solely for the general purpose of evaluating a possible transaction between the parties, that the Evaluation Material will be kept reasonably confidential and that Vector and its Representatives will endeavor not to disclose any of the Evaluation Material in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) Vector may make any disclosure of such information to which Captaris gives its subsequent consent and (ii) any of such information may be disclosed by Vector to its Representatives who seem to need such information for the general purpose of evaluating and implementing a possible transaction between the parties, who shall attempt to keep such information confidential and who may be provided with a copy of this letter agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces several ambiguities: "general purpose" is vague, "reasonably confidential" weakens the confidentiality requirement, "endeavor not to disclose" is not a strict prohibition, "subsequent consent" implies that consent is not needed upfront, "seem to need" introduces subjectivity, and "attempt to keep" is not a guarantee of confidentiality. The original text had a mandatory obligation and the modified text weakens the legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language, especially the phrases 'reasonably confidential' and 'endeavor not to disclose,' could be interpreted as failing to meet the standard of reasonable measures, thus potentially violating CUTSA if the Evaluation Material contains trade secrets and is subsequently disclosed due to the weakened confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the original legal obligations, potentially violating CUTSA as explained in the Law Explanation. Specifically, changing 'Evaluation Material will be kept confidential' to 'Evaluation Material will be kept reasonably confidential' and 'endeavor not to disclose' weakens the confidentiality requirement. The scraped law snippet 1 defines 'improper means' under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as including 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The weakening of the confidentiality obligation in the modified text could be interpreted as a failure to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation if the Evaluation Material contains trade secrets. The changes introduce enough ambiguity to create a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any event, Vector shall be responsible for any breach of this letter agreement by any of its Representatives, and Vector agrees, at its sole expense, to take reasonable measures (including but not limited to court proceedings) to restrain its Representatives from disclosure or use of the Evaluation Material in contravention of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In any event, Vector may be responsible for a potential breach of this letter agreement by its Representatives, and Vector agrees, at its discretion and expense, to consider measures to potentially restrain its Representatives from disclosure or use of the Evaluation Material that may contravene this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change weakens the responsibility by replacing 'shall be responsible' with 'may be responsible,' introducing uncertainty. 'At its sole expense' is replaced by 'at its discretion and expense' making it optional to pay. 'Reasonable measures' is replaced by 'consider measures' which makes the action optional, and 'potentially restrain' weakens the obligation to restrain. 'May contravene' introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While this agreement concerns confidentiality, weakening Vector's responsibility to prevent its representatives from using the Evaluation Material (which could include knowledge or skills) potentially allows those representatives to compete using information that should have been protected. By making restraint discretionary, Vector potentially enables its representatives to use information that could give them an unfair advantage, indirectly restraining competition, and shifting the burden of enforcement, thereby conflicting with the intent of the law to promote free competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Vector to take 'reasonable measures' to prevent its representatives from disclosing or using evaluation material. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating Vector 'may be responsible' and will 'consider measures to potentially restrain' representatives, introducing discretion where there was a clear responsibility. The Law Explanation suggests that this weakening could allow representatives to compete unfairly using protected information, conflicting with the intent of BPC 16600 to promote free competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. While the contract is not a direct non-compete, the alteration weakens Vector's responsibility to prevent unfair competition. The altered contract introduces ambiguities that would make the obligation more difficult to enforce under state law.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, except as required by law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization, each of Captaris and Vector agrees that, without the prior written consent of the other party, it and its Representatives will not disclose to any other person the fact that the Evaluation Material has been exchanged, that discussions or negotiations are or may be taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof) unless such first party has determined in good faith, after consultation with outside counsel, that such disclosure is required in order to avoid a violation of law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, except as vaguely suggested by law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization, each of Captaris and Vector believes that, without reasonable prior consent of the other party, it and its Representatives should not broadly disclose to most other persons the fact that Evaluation Material might have been exchanged, that discussions or negotiations could possibly be taking place concerning a hypothetical transaction involving the parties or some of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof) unless such first party feels in good faith, without thorough consultation with outside counsel, that such disclosure may be desired in order to avoid a potential violation of law or any rule of any self-regulatory organization.
perturbation.explanation: Multiple ambiguous changes were made. 'As required by law' was changed to 'vaguely suggested by law'. 'Agrees' was changed to 'believes'. 'Prior written consent' was changed to 'reasonable prior consent'. 'Will not disclose' was changed to 'should not broadly disclose'. 'Any other person' was changed to 'most other persons'. 'Are or may be' was changed to 'could possibly be'. 'Possible transaction' was changed to 'hypothetical transaction'. 'Some of the terms'. 'After consultation' was changed to 'without thorough consultation'. 'Is required' was changed to 'may be desired' and 'to avoid a violation' changed to 'avoid a potential violation'. This series of changes significantly weakens the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The original text aims to prevent premature disclosure of sensitive information about a potential transaction. The modified text, with its weakened language ('believes', 'should not broadly disclose', 'might have been exchanged', 'hypothetical transaction', 'potential violation', 'without thorough consultation') creates ambiguity and loopholes that could allow for selective or misleading disclosures. This could be used to improperly influence the market, contradicting the purpose of Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a clear duty to not disclose certain information. The changed text weakens this obligation through multiple ambiguous alterations, such as changing 'required by law' to 'vaguely suggested by law,' 'agrees' to 'believes,' and 'will not disclose' to 'should not broadly disclose.' These changes introduce ambiguity and discretion, potentially allowing parties to circumvent the original intent of non-disclosure. According to the Law Explanation, these changes contradict Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Snippet 1 mentions "15 USC 78j: Manipulative and deceptive devices", showing that the law aims to prevent deceptive practices, and the ambiguities introduced in the changed text go against the original intent of preventing premature disclosure, which the law seeks to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: In that case, or any time upon the request of a party for any reason, Vector will promptly, at Vector’s option (i) return to Captaris and/or (ii) destroy all Evaluation Material (and all tangible copies thereof) furnished to it or its Representatives by or on behalf of the other party pursuant hereto, and in either case no copies thereof shall be retained except that, any of the foregoing notwithstanding, that portion of the Evaluation Material that may be found in analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents prepared by Vector’s Representatives, such Representatives’ agents, attorneys or employees, and oral or electronic Evaluation Material will be held by such Representatives and kept subject to the terms of this agreement or destroyed to the extent practicable and permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: In that case, or at any time that is convenient upon a suggestion by a party for any reason, Vector will eventually, based on Vector’s preference, (i) consider returning to Captaris and/or (ii) attempt to destroy most of the Evaluation Material (and some of the tangible copies thereof) provided to it or its Representatives by or on behalf of the other party pursuant hereto, and in any case, a few copies thereof might be retained unless, any of the foregoing notwithstanding, that part of the Evaluation Material that is potentially contained in certain analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents prepared by Vector’s Representatives, some Representatives’ agents, attorneys or employees, and a bit of oral or electronic Evaluation Material may be held by such Representatives and maybe kept somewhat subject to the provisions of this agreement or somehow destroyed if potentially practicable and loosely permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: The changes were to weaken the original legal obligation. 'Promptly' changed to 'eventually'. 'At Vector's option' changed to 'based on Vector's preference'. 'Return' changed to 'consider returning'. 'Destroy all' changed to 'attempt to destroy most'. 'All tangible copies thereof' changed to 'some of the tangible copies thereof'. 'No copies thereof shall be retained' changed to 'a few copies thereof might be retained'. 'That portion' changed to 'that part', 'that may be found' changed to 'that is potentially contained'. 'Analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents' changed to 'certain analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents'. 'Oral or electronic evaluation material' changed to 'a bit of oral or electronic evaluation material'. 'Kept subject to the terms' changed to 'maybe kept somewhat subject to the terms', 'destroyed to the extent practicable' changed to 'somehow destroyed if potentially practicable', and 'permitted by law' changed to 'loosely permitted by law'. The new paragraph is not a strong legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for misappropriation of trade secrets. If the Evaluation Material contains trade secrets, the modified clause weakens the requirement to return or destroy it by including 'attempt to destroy most', 'some copies might be retained', and adding other ambiguity. This contradicts the spirit and intent of DTSA, which emphasizes protecting trade secrets through reasonable measures. A failure to 'promptly' and fully return or destroy such material could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect those trade secrets.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit of the DTSA by weakening the obligation to protect trade secrets. The original text requires 'prompt' action to 'return' or 'destroy' all Evaluation Material. The changed text replaces 'promptly' with 'eventually,' 'return' with 'consider returning,' and 'destroy all' with 'attempt to destroy most,' introducing ambiguity and potentially allowing for the retention of copies. This directly contradicts the DTSA's intent to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures, as indicated by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Civil proceedings' related to violations of trade secret protection, indicating the legal weight of protecting trade secrets, which the modified text undermines.
